title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing -- July 21, 2026
headline: Israel, Russia, and Sudan hold at the benchmark's floor of zero as fifteen reviews confirm every score.
summary: Fifteen governments, cities, and companies were reviewed overnight, and not one published score changed. Every result landed inside the benchmark's five-point confirmation range; the largest movement was a 3.4-point dip for Verizon. Three countries held at the benchmark's lowest possible score, zero out of 100. Israel's floor rests on continued lethal strikes on Gaza, including a July 17 strike on a funeral that killed seven and wounded twenty-two. Russia's floor rests on a July 15 glide-bomb strike on Sumy that killed five. The United Nations verified 65,044 civilian casualties in the Ukraine war, with a sharp rise this year. Sudan's floor rests on a months-long siege of the city of El Obeid by the Rapid Support Forces, trapping roughly 500,000 people and cutting off water service. The benchmark also has a standing rule: harm done to a country by someone else does not move that country's own score, up or down. Two reviews tested that rule tonight in opposite directions. Iran was reviewed after a US strike campaign killed more than 50 people on Iranian soil. The rule blocked any upgrade, since Iran was the target of that violence, not its source. Iran's score held at 2.5 out of 100. Palestine was reviewed after Israeli strikes and settler violence killed dozens of Palestinians. The same rule blocked any further downgrade, since Palestinian institutions did not cause that harm. Palestine's score held at 25 out of 100. Four governments and cities -- Azerbaijan, Algiers, Ibadan, and Tucson -- received a full, first-time individual review this cycle, replacing a flat starting score each had carried since before its first review. All four held close to their published scores, unlike several companies reviewed for the first time on July 20 that moved by ten points or more. Gabon and Pakistan both remain within three points of the boundary between the Critical and Developing bands. Both are being watched for the next event that could move them across it.

pipeline.queueHandling: No proposals were generated this cycle; the pending-changes queue carries forward unchanged.

topSignals.title: Israel, Russia, and Sudan Reconfirmed at the Benchmark's Absolute Floor of Zero
topSignals.whyItMatters: A score of zero is the benchmark's lowest possible reading. All three countries held there on fresh evidence dated this month, not on an old finding carried forward unchanged.
topSignals.description: Israel's floor reflects continued lethal strikes on Gaza. A July 17 strike on a funeral killed seven people and wounded twenty-two, part of a toll of at least 1,123 people killed since the ceasefire began in October 2025. Russia's floor reflects a July 15 glide-bomb strike on the city of Sumy that killed five people, alongside a United Nations finding of 65,044 verified civilian casualties in the Ukraine war, with a sharp rise so far this year. Sudan's floor reflects a months-long siege of the city of El Obeid by the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces. Roughly 500,000 people are trapped, and the city's water system has been bombed. The European Parliament voted 476 to 28 this month to designate the Rapid Support Forces a terrorist organization. A score of zero cannot go lower, so this cycle's new evidence confirms rather than changes the placement.

evidence.claim: A July 17 strike on a funeral in Gaza killed seven people and wounded twenty-two, continuing a pattern of civilian harm since the October 2025 ceasefire.
evidence.source: OCHA (UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs)

evidence.claim: A July 15 glide-bomb strike on Sumy, Ukraine killed five people; the UN separately verified 65,044 civilian casualties in the war with a sharp rise in 2026.
The Rapid Support Forces' siege of El Obeid has trapped roughly 500,000 people and destroyed water infrastructure; the European Parliament voted 476-28 to designate the RSF a terrorist organization.

topSignals.title: The Benchmark's Own Rule Was Tested Twice Tonight: Harm Done to a Country Does Not Move Its Score
topSignals.whyItMatters: A country that is attacked by someone else should not see its own compassion score rise or fall because of that attack. Two reviews tonight tested this rule in opposite directions, and it held both times.
topSignals.description: Iran was reviewed after a US strike campaign killed more than 50 people on Iranian soil. Because Iran was the target of that violence rather than its source, the benchmark's screening rule blocked any upgrade based on it. Iran's score held at 2.5 out of 100, a placement that rests on the country's own record of executions -- 2,159 in 2025, about 80 percent of the world's known total. Palestine was reviewed after Israeli strikes and settler violence killed dozens of Palestinians between July 8 and July 15, including six journalists. Because Palestinian institutions did not cause that harm, the same rule blocked any further downgrade. Palestine's score held at 25 out of 100.

evidence.claim: Iran recorded 2,159 executions in 2025, about 80 percent of the world's known total, sustaining its near-floor placement independent of this month's strikes.

OCHA reported 40 people killed and 127 injured in the Occupied Palestinian Territory between July 8 and July 15, including six journalists, and more than 3,200 people displaced by settler violence in the West Bank.
evidence.source: OCHA (UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs)

topSignals.title: Verizon's Layoffs Drove Tonight's Largest Score Movement, But Its Band Held
topSignals.whyItMatters: A company can keep cutting jobs quarter after quarter without its score crossing a line, as long as the total movement stays under the benchmark's five-point confirmation threshold.
topSignals.description: Verizon's score fell 3.4 points, from 48.4 to a confirmed 45 out of 100, the largest single movement of the night. On July 16, Verizon cut 274 retail stores and laid off 500 more corporate employees, bringing its total job cuts since October 2025 to more than 16,600, even as the company reported roughly $5 billion in quarterly profit. That pattern lowered Verizon's Integrity score specifically. It was not enough to move the company out of the Functional band, because Verizon Frontline disaster-response work and digital-inclusion programs for low-income households offset part of the decline. The reviewers flagged Verizon's remaining scores as a flat starting-point estimate that has never been individually tested, the same gap that produced larger movements at several other companies reviewed for the first time on July 20.

evidence.claim: Verizon will close 274 stores and lay off 500 more corporate employees, bringing job cuts since October 2025 past 16,600, even as the company posted roughly $5 billion in quarterly profit.

topSignals.title: Four Governments Got Their First Full Review This Cycle, and Three Held Almost Exactly Where They Started
topSignals.whyItMatters: When a placeholder score meets real scrutiny for the first time, it can move sharply in either direction. Tonight's first-time reviews mostly did not, which is itself informative.
topSignals.description: Azerbaijan, Algiers, Ibadan, and Tucson each received a full, first-time 40-part review this cycle, replacing a flat starting score assigned by category rather than by individual research. Azerbaijan held in the Critical band at 8.1, close to its published 9.4, despite renewed border violence with Armenia and a documented crackdown ahead of a constitutional referendum. Algiers held in the Developing band at 21.3 after a July 16 orphanage fire killed 11 people; reviewers weighed that single fire against Algeria's national civil-protection system, which logged 913 emergency responses since July 8. Ibadan held in the Critical band at 17.5; a deadly ethnic clash on July 8 was one data point inside a municipal profile with genuinely limited service capacity, not the sole driver of the score. Tucson held in the Developing band at 35 despite a July 19 mass shooting that wounded ten people, offset by a city Housing First program that has served 3,322 people and housed 799 since 2021.

evidence.claim: A fire at an orphanage on the outskirts of Algiers killed 11 people, including children, and injured 19, during a heatwave that had already prompted 913 fire responses nationwide since July 8.
A mass shooting in downtown Tucson on July 19 wounded ten people; the city's Housing First program has served 3,322 people and housed 799 since October 2021.
evidence.source: US News & World Report / City of Tucson

topSignals.title: Gabon and Pakistan Both Sit Within Three Points of the Line Between Critical and Developing
topSignals.whyItMatters: A small number of points separates each country from crossing into a different published band. Neither moved tonight, but both are close enough that the next dated event could change that.
topSignals.description: Gabon held at 20.0, just above the Critical-band line at 20, after reviewers weighed a July 20 request for French intervention over a jailed opposition leader against Gabon's post-coup transitional governance record. Pakistan held at 16.3, inside the Critical band and 2.8 points below the line into Developing, after the death toll from Operation Shaaban, a security operation in Balochistan, reached 91 people by July 16. That toll is still reported only by security sources, now in its fifth consecutive week without independent verification.

evidence.claim: Gabon's opposition sought French intervention on July 20 over the continued detention of an opposition leader, against a backdrop of a 94.85 percent presidential election result under transitional governance.
evidence.source: Bertelsmann Transformation Index (BTI) 2026 Gabon country report

evidence.claim: The death toll from Pakistan's Operation Shaaban in Balochistan reached 91 by July 16, reported only by security sources for a fifth consecutive week.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A further escalation against the detained opposition leader, or a documented French response to the July 20 intervention request, is the next event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Gabon sits at 20.3 of 100, 0.3 points above the Critical band. This cycle's evidence -- a July 20 request for French intervention over a jailed opposition leader -- was consistent with the published score and did not move it.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented change in Oyo State's de-escalation follow-through after the July 8 Ojoo clash is the next event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Ibadan sits at 18.8 of 100, 1.2 points below the Developing band. This cycle's first full review found genuinely limited municipal capacity behind the score, not a one-off event.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A further heatwave-driven emergency, or a documented failure of Algeria's national civil-protection response, is the next event that would move this score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Algiers sits at 21.9 of 100, 1.9 points above the Critical band. The July 16 orphanage fire was weighed against Algeria's national fire-response capacity, which held the score steady.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent, non-security-source verification of the Balochistan operation's civilian toll is the next event that would move this score.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. The Operation Shaaban death toll reached 91 by July 16, still reported only by security sources into a fifth consecutive week.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented change in Egypt's refugee-deportation practice, or a further Human Rights Watch or UNHCR finding, is the next event that would test this score.
boundaryWatch.note: Egypt sits at 21.9 of 100, 1.9 points above the Critical band. Not assessed this cycle; the July 20 finding carries forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A verified, in-window escalation or de-escalation in labor-reform enforcement is the next event that would move this score across the Functional boundary.
boundaryWatch.note: Bangladesh sits at 39.8 of 100, 0.2 points below the Functional band. Not assessed this cycle; carried forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A worsening of the UN-backed hunger alert without a matching expansion of health-insurance or school-enrolment coverage would be the next event to move this score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Djibouti sits at 20.3 of 100, 0.3 points above the Critical band. Not assessed this cycle; carried forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented use of the extended presidential term, or a contested 2030 election process, would be the next event to test this score.
boundaryWatch.note: Zimbabwe sits at 20.3 of 100, 0.3 points above the Critical band. Not assessed this cycle; carried forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented own-conduct deterioration in epidemic response, such as government obstruction of WHO response corridors, would be the next event that could push Uganda back into the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Uganda sits at 20.3 of 100, 0.3 points above the Critical band. Not assessed this cycle; carried forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. Not assessed this cycle; carried forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed this cycle; carried forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the removed tracker was broader or retained longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each independently move the score toward a change. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. Not assessed this cycle; carried forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. Not assessed this cycle; carried forward unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. Not assessed this cycle. The calibration question against Burkina Faso has now been open roughly twenty-eight consecutive days, with no resolution timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed this cycle. The calibration question against Mali remains open roughly twenty-eight consecutive days.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Bolivia's conduct profile, as an elected government under economic and civil-unrest strain, against Critical-band peers facing state collapse or mass atrocity is the next event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Bolivia sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed this cycle; the calibration question remains open, now about ten days since the flag was opened July 11.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. This cycle's evidence -- Israeli strikes and settler violence killing dozens and displacing thousands -- was screened as harm inflicted upon Palestine, not caused by it, and did not move the score.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented mass-execution event or formal international atrocity finding is the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.
boundaryWatch.note: Iran sits at 2.5 of 100, 2.5 points above the absolute floor. This cycle's US strike campaign is logged as harm inflicted on Iran, not scored against it.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. The Ebola outbreak's death toll reached 930 this cycle, still described as the fastest-growing on record.

evidence.claim: More than 10,000 protesters marching on the Indian Parliament over an alleged testing scandal were met with tear gas and baton charges on July 20; Delhi's municipal government separately runs 13 hospitals and 102 maternal and child welfare centers.

recentAssessments.whyHeadline: A first full review found genuinely limited municipal capacity behind an ethnic-clash-triggered score.

nextForwardSignal.label: A documented change in Oyo State's de-escalation follow-through after the July 8 Ojoo clash is the next event to watch.

recentAssessments.whyHeadline: A July 19 mass shooting was weighed against Tucson's genuine Housing First infrastructure.

evidence.claim: A downtown Tucson mass shooting on July 19 wounded ten people; officers on foot patrol engaged the shooter quickly.
Tucson's Housing First program has served 3,322 people and housed 799 since October 2021, across a 50-organization collaboration.
evidence.source: City of Tucson, Department of Housing and Community Development

recentAssessments.whyHeadline: Layoffs topped 16,600 since October 2025 despite roughly $5 billion in quarterly profit.

nextForwardSignal.label: A further disclosed round of Verizon job cuts is the next event that could push this score toward a five-point change.

evidence.claim: Verizon will close 274 stores and lay off 500 more corporate employees, bringing job cuts since October 2025 past 16,600, even as the company posted roughly $5 billion in quarterly profit.

emergingRisks.risk: Verizon's job cuts have now reached 16,600 since October 2025, a sustained trend rather than a single event. A further disclosed round could push the score toward a five-point threshold crossing in a future cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: Tonight's 3.4-point movement stayed inside the confirmation range, but the underlying layoff pattern is monotonic across nine consecutive months.

emergingRisks.risk: The scan's own prioritization formula again ranked several single-incident cities and one company above eight ongoing-conflict countries tied at the same priority score, though less severely than the prior cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: Recently-assessed conflict countries lose priority ranking to never-recently-assessed single-incident entities under the current staleness-based formula. No corporate or university entity outranked an active-conflict country this cycle, an improvement on the prior cycle.

emergingRisks.risk: The Mali-Burkina Faso scoring-consistency question is now roughly twenty-eight consecutive days open, the benchmark's longest-running unresolved item.
emergingRisks.detail: Neither country was assessed this cycle; the underlying question of whether the two countries are scored consistently for comparable government conduct remains open with no resolution timeline set.

emergingRisks.risk: The Bolivia critical-band calibration question remains open alongside the longer-running Mali-Burkina Faso question.
emergingRisks.detail: The question is whether Bolivia's score is calibrated consistently against Critical-band peers facing very different conditions, such as state collapse or mass atrocity.

sectorAlerts.sector: Countries -- Three Absolute-Floor Confirmations and Two Screening-Rule Tests
sectorAlerts.headline: Israel, Russia, and Sudan held at zero on fresh atrocity evidence, while Iran and Palestine held after screening rule 3 blocked harm-driven score movement in each direction.
observations: Israel, Russia, and Sudan (all 0.0 of 100): each floor rested on a newly-dated event this cycle rather than carried-forward documentation.
Iran (2.5) and Palestine (25.0): both surfaced on harm inflicted by an external actor; screening rule 3 blocked an upgrade for Iran and a further downgrade for Palestine.
Azerbaijan (8.1), Gabon (20.0), Pakistan (16.3), and Armenia (46.9) all confirmed within threshold across the Critical, Developing, and Functional bands.

sectorAlerts.sector: Global Cities -- First Full Reviews Mostly Hold Steady
sectorAlerts.headline: Algiers and Ibadan received first full individual reviews and held close to their published scores; Delhi's protest-crackdown evidence was weighed against genuine municipal service infrastructure.
observations: Algiers (21.3): a July 16 orphanage fire that killed 11 was weighed against Algeria's national civil-protection response capacity.
Ibadan (17.5): a July 8 ethnic clash was one data point inside a municipal profile with genuinely limited service capacity.
Delhi (31.9): a July 20 protest crackdown targeted the national government, not primarily the municipal government being assessed.

sectorAlerts.sector: US Cities -- A Mass Shooting Weighed Against Real Care Infrastructure
sectorAlerts.headline: Tucson held its published score after a mass shooting was weighed against a documented Housing First program.
observations: Tucson (35.0): a July 19 downtown shooting that wounded ten people did not outweigh a Housing First program that has served 3,322 people and housed 799 since 2021.

sectorAlerts.sector: Fortune 500 -- Verizon's Layoffs Produce the Night's Largest Movement
sectorAlerts.headline: Verizon's score fell 3.4 points on continued layoffs, the largest single movement of the night, without crossing out of the Functional band.
observations: Verizon (45.0): job cuts since October 2025 have reached 16,600, alongside roughly $5 billion in quarterly profit; the layoffs lowered Verizon's Integrity score specifically.

methodologyNotes.name: Harm Done to an Institution Is Not Evidence About That Institution's Own Conduct
methodologyNotes.description: The benchmark scores institutions on what they do, not on what happens to them. Tonight, Iran was reviewed after a US strike campaign killed people on Iranian soil, and Palestine was reviewed after Israeli strikes and settler violence killed Palestinians. In both cases, the harm was inflicted by an outside actor, not caused by the institution being scored, so neither score moved. This rule was tested in opposite directions -- blocking an upgrade for one entity and a downgrade for the other -- in the same review cycle.

methodologyNotes.name: A Single Incident Is Weighed Against a City's Full Record, Not Treated as the Whole Score
methodologyNotes.description: When a city government is reviewed after one dated tragic or contentious event -- a fire, a mass shooting, an ethnic clash, a protest crackdown -- that event is treated as one data point among the benchmark's 40 measured areas, not as the entire assessment. Tonight, four cities and one country each had exactly one such event in the review window, and in every case the event was weighed against the government's broader service infrastructure rather than allowed to dominate the score.

forwardTriggers.trigger: A further disclosed round of Verizon job cuts, on top of the 16,600 since October 2025, is the next event that could push this score toward a five-point change.
A further escalation against the detained opposition leader, or a documented French response to the July 20 intervention request.
Independent, non-security-source verification of the Balochistan operation's civilian toll.
Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization Ebola response corridors. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
A coordinator-level comparison of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable government conduct. Now roughly twenty-eight days open with no set date.
The same comparison, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.
A comparison of Bolivia's score against Critical-band peers facing very different conditions. No timeline set.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Iran and Palestine both held their published scores tonight after screening rule 3 blocked the harm-driven signal in each case -- barring an upgrade for Iran, a downgrade for Palestine. Does one rule constitute a symmetric standard across a sovereign state and a non-state territory, or would future evidence test each differently?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Two entities on opposite sides of the country index shared the same evidentiary condition tonight -- harm inflicted by an outside actor -- and the same rule held both in place.
